reference: GATE CS — Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

map.condition: Counting bus transfers / micro-ops in single-bus datapath

map.use: W_{\text{horiz}} = N_{\text{control signals}} + N_{\text{cond bits}} + N_{\text{branch addr bits}}

map.condition: Minimum bits when at most k signals active simultaneously

formulas.name: Micro-operation sequence on single bus
formulas.plain: MAR <- PC; MDR <- M[MAR]; IR <- MDR; PC <- PC + 1  (fetch cycle, 4 micro-ops on single bus)
formulas.whenToUse: Counting the minimum number of bus cycles to fetch an instruction in a single-bus CPU
formulas.trap: On a single bus only one transfer can occur per clock; MAR and MDR always mediate memory access — do not skip them.

formulas.name: ALU execute micro-operation (register-register)
formulas.latex: T_1: \text{A} \leftarrow R_s \quad T_2: \text{B} \leftarrow R_t \quad T_3: R_d \leftarrow \text{A} \oplus \text{B}
formulas.plain: T1: A <- Rs; T2: B <- Rt; T3: Rd <- A op B  (three bus cycles for ALU R-R op)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining the number of clock steps for a register-register ALU operation on a single-bus datapath with temporary registers A and B
formulas.trap: The ALU cannot read two registers in one step on a single bus; temporary registers A and B must be loaded one at a time.

formulas.name: PUSH instruction micro-operations (single-bus)
formulas.plain: MAR <- SP; MDR <- R; M[MAR] <- MDR; SP <- SP - 1
formulas.whenToUse: Writing the micro-operation sequence for a PUSH instruction in a register-based, single-bus CPU
formulas.trap: Some CPUs decrement SP before the write (pre-decrement); others after (post-decrement). Read the ISA specification carefully.

formulas.name: Memory-read micro-operations
formulas.plain: MAR <- addr; MDR <- M[MAR]; Rd <- MDR
formulas.whenToUse: Counting bus steps for a LOAD instruction on a single-bus datapath

terms.means: Effective address (may require additional computation steps)

formulas.trap: The memory-read latency (MDR <- M[MAR]) is one step but can span multiple clock cycles depending on memory speed.

formulas.name: PC always points to NEXT instruction
formulas.plain: PC = address of next instruction to fetch (not the currently executing one)
formulas.whenToUse: Any question about the value of PC during or after instruction execution
formulas.trap: A common distractor states 'PC points to the last instruction executed' — this is FALSE. PC is incremented during fetch to point to the next instruction.

formulas.name: Stack — LIFO discipline
formulas.plain: Stack operates as LIFO: the last element pushed is the first element popped
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about the operating principle of the hardware stack (used for CALL/RET, PUSH/POP)
formulas.trap: Do not confuse with queue (FIFO). Hardware stacks grow toward lower addresses on most architectures.

formulas.name: Conditional JUMP/CALL does NOT affect flags
formulas.latex: \text{Conditional JUMP/CALL: reads flags, does NOT modify them}
formulas.plain: Conditional jump or call instructions test the flags register but do not alter it
formulas.whenToUse: True/False questions about whether conditional branch instructions modify the flags register

terms.means: Status/condition-code register bits (Z, N, C, V)

formulas.trap: FALSE: conditional jumps/calls do not set flags. Flags are set only by arithmetic, logical, and compare instructions.

formulas.name: Instruction prefetch / pipeline speedup
formulas.plain: Prefetch hides memory latency by fetching instruction i+1 while instruction i executes
formulas.whenToUse: Explaining why instruction prefetching / instruction buffers speed up execution
formulas.trap: Speedup is reduced or eliminated when branches occur, because prefetched instructions may be wrong-path instructions.

formulas.name: Instruction cycle phases
formulas.plain: Fetch -> Decode -> EA -> Fetch Operand -> Execute -> Store Result
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying which phase performs a specific action (e.g., which phase reads from memory for a LOAD)
formulas.trap: Not all phases occur for every instruction type; memory-reference instructions have more phases than register-register instructions.

formulas.name: Horizontal microinstruction word width
formulas.plain: W_horiz = (number of control signals) + (condition select bits) + (next-address bits)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing total bit-width of a horizontal (unencoded) microinstruction

terms.means: One bit per control signal (no encoding)
Bits to select among conditions for conditional branch
Bits to address the control memory (= ceil(log2(size)))

formulas.trap: Horizontal means one bit per signal — no encoding. Do not take log of the control signals for the horizontal format.

formulas.name: Vertical (fully encoded) microinstruction word width
formulas.plain: W_vert = ceil(log2(N_ctrl + 1)) + cond_bits + next_addr_bits
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the minimum control word width when all signals are fully encoded into a single field
formulas.trap: Full encoding minimises word width but requires a decoder, increasing latency — it is slower than horizontal microprogramming.

formulas.name: Next-address field width in control word
formulas.plain: next_addr_bits = ceil(log2(control_memory_depth))
formulas.whenToUse: Finding how many bits are needed to address the control store
formulas.trap: The next-address bits must be enough to address every microinstruction, so always round UP with ceiling.

formulas.name: Condition-select bits for conditional branching
formulas.plain: cond_bits = ceil(log2(number_of_conditions))
formulas.whenToUse: Computing how many bits select among multiple branch conditions in a microinstruction
formulas.trap: Include the 'unconditional branch' and 'no-branch' cases when counting total conditions.

formulas.name: Minimum bits when at most k signals active simultaneously
formulas.plain: bits = ceil(log2(sum of C(N,i) for i=0 to k))  where N = total signals, k = max simultaneously active
formulas.whenToUse: When at most k out of N control signals can be active at once — find the minimum encoding width

terms.means: Number of ways to choose i active signals from N

formulas.trap: For k=1 this reduces to ceil(log2(N+1)). For k=2, N=25: C(25,0)+C(25,1)+C(25,2) = 1+25+300 = 326, so ceil(log2 326) = 9 bits.

formulas.name: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed control comparison
formulas.plain: Hardwired control: faster execution, not easily modified.  Microprogrammed: easier to add new instructions, slower due to control-memory access.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions comparing speed and flexibility of hardwired vs microprogrammed control units

terms.means: Logic-circuit-based control; fast but costly to modify
Control signals stored in control memory (ROM/RAM)

formulas.trap: A common GATE trap: 'microprogrammed is faster than hardwired' is FALSE. Hardwired is faster; microprogrammed is more flexible.

formulas.name: Control memory (microprogram store) size
formulas.plain: Control Memory Size (bits) = number_of_microinstructions x microword_width_in_bits
formulas.whenToUse: Calculating total size of the microprogram store

terms.means: Number of microinstructions (rows in control store)
Width of each microinstruction in bits

formulas.trap: Do not confuse control memory (stores microinstructions) with main memory (stores instructions and data).

formulas.name: Microinstruction vs Machine instruction hierarchy
formulas.plain: Each machine instruction is decoded into a sequence of microinstructions that generate control signals
formulas.whenToUse: Understanding what a microprogrammed control unit does when it receives a machine instruction

terms.means: Stored sequence of microinstructions implementing one machine instruction
Specifies control signals for one micro-step

formulas.trap: Microprogramming is NOT about running small user programs; it is about implementing the CPU's own control logic.

formulas.name: Horizontal vs Vertical microinstruction format
formulas.latex: \text{Horizontal: wide word, parallel control, fast} \quad \text{Vertical: narrow word, encoded, needs decoder}
formulas.plain: Horizontal: 1 bit per control line — wide but fast; Vertical: encoded fields — compact but needs a decoder, slower.
formulas.whenToUse: Distinguishing the two microinstruction encoding styles and their trade-offs

terms.means: Unencoded; each bit directly drives one control line
Encoded; decoder between microword and control lines

formulas.trap: Horizontal words are WIDER but generate control signals FASTER (no decoder delay). Vertical words are NARROWER but SLOWER.

formulas.name: Wilkes-style microprogram sequencing
formulas.plain: Next microinstruction address = microPC+1 (sequential) or branch_address (branch micro-op)
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing execution flow through a microprogram or computing how branching is encoded
formulas.trap: The branch-address field in the microword must be wide enough to reach any microinstruction in the control store.

formulas.name: Standard ALU operations
formulas.plain: ALU operations: ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR, CMP (compare sets flags without storing result)
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying which operations the ALU can perform and which unit handles them

terms.means: Compare — performs subtraction, sets flags, discards result

formulas.trap: Multiplication and division are typically NOT in the basic ALU; they use separate multiply/divide units or multi-cycle sequences.

formulas.name: Accumulator-based vs GPR-based data path
formulas.plain: Accumulator: one implicit operand in ACC; GPR (general-purpose register): all operands in named registers
formulas.whenToUse: Answering questions about instruction formats and the number of address fields in an instruction

terms.means: Accumulator register — implicit source and destination

formulas.trap: Accumulator machines have shorter instructions (fewer address fields) but require more memory accesses; GPR machines are more flexible.
